name: Ojos del Cabriel and cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular by La Herrería Waterfall
name_original: Ojos del Cabriel y cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular por la Cascada de La Herrería
region_intro: Jogging around El Vallecillo offers routes through a natural environment characterized by high-altitude pine forests, ranging from 4,600 to 5,250 feet (1,400 to 1,600 meters). The landscape features the source of the River Cabriel, which carves picturesque valleys, and notable waterfalls like the Cascada del Molino de San Pedro. Karstic reliefs, including sinkholes and chasms, contribute to the varied terrain, providing diverse options for runners. Best jogging routes around El Vallecillo The most popular jogging route is Ojos del Cabriel and cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular by La Herrería Waterfall, a 4.6 miles (7.5 km) trail that takes 54 minutes to complete. This route explores the source of the Cabriel River and passes by scenic waterfalls. Another top favourite among local runners is Mirador del Alto de las Peñas – circular from Terriente, a moderate 6.7 miles (10.7 km) path. This route offers elevated views and winds through the region's characteristic pine forests. Local runners also love the Fuente del Buey Local Trail, an 8.1 miles (13.0 km) trail leading through diverse forest terrain, often completed in about 1 hour 29 minutes. Jogging around El Vallecillo is defined by high-altitude pine forests, the River Cabriel, and scenic waterfalls. The network offers options for different ability levels, with all routes currently listed as moderate. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 10 runners have used komoot to explore El Vallecillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in El Vallecillo?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;5&lt;/strong&gt; dedicated running routes around El Vallecillo, all of which are rated as moderate difficulty. These trails offer diverse experiences through the region's unique natural landscape.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails in El Vallecillo?
A: The running trails in El Vallecillo primarily traverse high-altitude pine forests, ranging from 1,400 to 1,600 meters (approximately 4,600 to 5,250 feet). You'll find paths along the picturesque Cabriel River, through areas with karstic reliefs like sinkholes, and past stunning waterfalls. The varied terrain offers both gentle sections and some challenging ascents.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by waterfalls?
A: Yes, several routes feature beautiful waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983156660/ojos-del-cabriel-and-cascada-del-molino-de-san-pedro-circular-by-la-herreria-waterfall" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel and cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular by La Herrería Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; route is an excellent choice, as it takes you past both the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/604533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Pedro Mill Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247809" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Herrería Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in El Vallecillo?
A: Absolutely. Many of the running routes in El Vallecillo are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983156660/ojos-del-cabriel-and-cascada-del-molino-de-san-pedro-circular-by-la-herreria-waterfall" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel and cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular by La Herrería Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983323510/mirador-del-alto-de-las-penas-circular-from-terriente" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Alto de las Peñas – circular from Terriente&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983328144/pino-monumental-running-circular-from-moscardon-through-prado-molinero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pino Monumental – running circular from Moscardón through Prado Molinero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural landmarks can I discover on a run in El Vallecillo?
A: Beyond the stunning waterfalls, you can explore the official source of the Cabriel River, known as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also pass by the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547205" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monumental Pine&lt;/a&gt;, and you might encounter the unique karstic reliefs characteristic of the region's geology.

Q: Are there any running trails that offer elevated viewpoints?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983323510/mirador-del-alto-de-las-penas-circular-from-terriente" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Alto de las Peñas – circular from Terriente&lt;/a&gt; route is specifically designed to provide elevated views, winding through the region's characteristic pine forests and offering scenic vistas.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in El Vallecillo?
A: Given its high-altitude location (1,400 to 1,600 meters), El Vallecillo offers a cool and shaded environment, making it ideal for running, especially during warmer months. The pine forests provide natural cover, enhancing the experience.

Q: Are there any longer distance running trails for more experienced runners?
A: While all routes are currently rated as moderate, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983315048/fuente-del-buey-local-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Buey Local Trail&lt;/a&gt; is the longest at 8.1 miles (13.0 km), offering a more extended experience through diverse forest terrain for those looking for a longer run.

Q: What makes El Vallecillo a unique place for outdoor running?
A: El Vallecillo stands out for its unique combination of high-altitude pine forests, the pristine source of the Cabriel River, and dramatic karstic landscapes. A significant portion of the area is part of a Site of Community Importance (LIC) and Special Protection Area (ZEPA), ensuring a well-preserved natural environment for your runs.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about the trails in El Vallecillo?
A: Runners frequently praise the diverse natural beauty, from the tranquil pine forests to the captivating waterfalls and the source of the Cabriel River. The well-maintained infrastructure, including metal walkways on some routes, also contributes to an enjoyable and accessible running experience.

Q: Are there any trails that explore the source of the Cabriel River?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983156660/ojos-del-cabriel-and-cascada-del-molino-de-san-pedro-circular-by-la-herreria-waterfall" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel and cascada del Molino de San Pedro – circular by La Herrería Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; route specifically explores the official source of the Cabriel River, known as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4547251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojos del Cabriel&lt;/a&gt;.
tips:
The water springs that supply the river, in this case the Cabriel, are known as Ojos. The water springs from the ground and in this area there are two of them very close. It is reached by a very entertaining path without technical difficulty.

** The water springs that supply the river, in this case the Cabriel, are known as Ojos. The water springs from the ground and in this area there are two of them very close. It is reached by a very entertaining path without technical difficulty

The Cascada de la Herrería is a natural gem located in the Sierra de Albarracín, near the town of El Vallecillo. This beautiful waterfall, also known as the “Hidden Waterfall,” has a drop of approximately 8 meters over the Cabriel River. 
To reach the waterfall, you can follow a hiking route that begins in El Vallecillo. The route is short, just half a kilometer, and takes you through a varied and picturesque landscape. During the walk, you will also be able to see the natural springs known as the Ojos del Cabriel, which feed the river and the waterfall.

Without a doubt one of the most beautiful waterfalls with a pool I have been to in Spain. Its location, in what is called "emptied Spain" due to its low population density, has prevented its overcrowding, although in summer there were quite a few people bathing. It is advisable to wear swimming or canyoning shoes to enter and exit the water through the porous stones. The view from the cavity behind the waterfall is simply stunning.
